author: @ClownWhisper
text:
It actually breaks my heart because it reminds me of where I come from in my adult life I worked for a niche that is start up companies that do Innovation for Pharmaceutical sector towards the end of drug discovery it's hard to describe LOL basically I work for many companies that develop robotics platforms that would automate different processes that would Aid in Expediting drug Discovery. Once we hit Wall Street once we became a publicly traded company it was all about the bottom line it was no longer about the science at all. For a bunch of people that are trying to make the world a better place and help people live healthier longer lives to all of a sudden be put in a position of solely making money through appearances and I'm sure you know what that means it's heartbreaking.

Our company which I will not name started to buy other companies up for the sole purpose of playing a dream I'm Wall Street a very destructive game that over the years has been made legal through manipulation of regulations. We would buy a company that had absolutely fantastic products I mean groundbreaking drug Discovery Dynamo products that could change the World As We Knew It but it was more important to slash and burn and make all the Insiders a bunch of money.

The game goes like this

You buy a company that may be struggling a little bit you blow smoke up their asses buy them out and then instead of trying to streamline their operations to save a little bit of money and make their products better we would just completely devastate their engineering staff because that's where the largest percentage of the money goes so we'd fire all but maybe one or two engineers. When fire anybody that wasn't absolutely essential for day-to-day operations just to maintain the appearance open operational facility. So what happens when this occurs? Within this first couple months of firing everyone you build up inventory you push operations to its limit and you put a bunch of stock in storage. And then that next quarter it looks like you are in the black with a brand new facility under your belt and everybody Pfizer stock it goes through the roof even though you have no way of supporting products because there's no engineering staff left and by the time everyone realizes it all of the bigwigs have sold off their shares at a gigantic profit. At that point they sell off intellectual property and physical assets about 3/4 down the road to make another group of people that do due diligence properly when they're buying stocks to make them think you're making more money and so a second round of glutton occurs. Eventually everybody is laid off the doors are closed and all remaining assets are sold off and you move on to the next company. We did this for God damn 20 years and it was the most disgusting thing that I've ever seen in my life it hurt the pharmaceutical industry it hurt the population of this world and it's sickening I hate Wall Street and what it's become

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
It must be very difficult to do research and development on a shoe string budget, particularly in the current financial climate and when competing with companies that have huge resources at their disposal.
There is no doubt that there is a future for anyone who comes up with an energy storage system that addresses the needs of moving away from fossil fuels and towards an electric powered green energy future. I would like to know how you are progressing in characterising your carbon battery. Do you publish the specification of the cell? It must be difficult to obtain tolerance and ageing characteristics on such a small number of hand made samples. 
I thought you were making great progress when you replaced the battery in that electric motor bike. At that point it looked as if you would have a product on the market in a very short time. There must be a market for an environmentally friendly battery, that is based on low cost carbon technology.
Just watching your videos it is difficult to judge the performance of your battery or how the performance is improving with each iteration. Am I correct in assuming that you do not publish the complete specification for commercial reasons? Is there any chance you will be publishing a full data sheet any time soon?
By full, I mean energy density, charge/discharge curves with temperature, number of recharge cycles, recharge efficiency, battery life. I know you have produced videos on each of these characteristics, but I do not think you have published a complete set in the form of a full data sheet on single size of battery.

Have you considered producing a technical sales video that covers the fully specification of a standard size version of your battery?

author: @DKTAz00
text: Companies, in the power sector, reeeeeaaally want a BIG, CHEAP battery. Capacity is secondary, as you can put them on any kind of land thus space is not a factor. Figure out a way to scale the production of a battery, 5 times the size of one of those large oil storage tanks a gas plant has on site, and you could roughly store about half of the energy that would be in one tank of oil. Reason for all this, when the wind blows hard, electricity prices become negative. They can only store most of that power as heat, and heat is worth next to nothing. As its a byproduct of coal/gas, which we use when the wind dosnt blow. So batteries, passive, low maintenance, can make big dollars. Edit* really big money, if you only supply power during peak periods. (electricity is traded by the hour)

author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
text:
Hi Rob.....I would describe myself as the average person who watches your vids mainly for entertainment. I found your channel whilst searching for off grid battery ideas/concepts as, again I would guess, many others have done also. I’ve been listening to you on this vid while scrolling through all of the current comments and wondered how I (the average person) could help/get involved/get something out of it at the same time. Ignore me if I’ve missed the point or I’m being too simplistic here but here’s my thoughts.......it seems like you have the know-how but not the funds or time to mass produce/ramp up production (buying materials, paying for labour etc). I’m construction industry related - could you not do what several self-builders have done (very successfully) and offer, let’s say, a complete battery to anyone who could give up their own free time for you to use in any way you required (eg instead of helping to build a house you use them to build other batteries while you supervise)....in this way you not only get increased output but get more WORKING batteries out there which, by word of mouth/seeing is believing, can only generate masses of interest from, at least, others like me who dream of off-grid solar storage? A persons free time is exchanged for them getting a working battery, you get a significant increase in output and free marketing. Word of mouth is a fantastic way of exponentially expanding an interest in something EVERYONE can find a use for. I, for one (remember I’m joe public) would jump at the chance of volunteering my time/services because the incentive is to get a working battery. Don’t get me wrong I’m not talking about a complete battery bank here as that limit would be set by you........just my thoughts.....good luck anyway.....
Edit.....swap building/getting a free battery for producing/getting a free KIT (ties in with a lot other comments)

author: @beamer.electronics
text: I empathise greatly with what you're doing - it's like wading through mud. I've been working on ideas for the last 45 years few were successful and most failed. I found (with low resources), complicated products/ideas usually fail, and very simple (well within one's resources), succeed. Your inks are a good foundation stone but, you need to find at least another unique and simple idea - that sells in it's many thousands. Anything to do with CE will cost big bucks - we really need an open compliance resource here. Time/money spent on patents - mostly wasted. Serious advertising - not a good return. What we are left with is probably a kit, but a kit that nigh-on everybody truly wants? IMHO it's also a mistake thinking one knows what the market needs - the fickle market place is far removed from a creators mind. Perhaps, spend money on a reputable and finger-on-the-pulse marketing advisor? Finally, what's needed is (at a governmental level), proper recognition that clever people (as yourself), are encouraged, given the support and resources to grow. Or they will (and do), sadly drown under the weight of this stuff! The UK really needs to start making wanted (quality), things again - you get my vote mate :)

replies.text: I agree mate - real value is iin manufacture - the market is about moving money around - mostly from the small investor to the big corp

text: That very thin, flat (and flexible) Form Factor hints at some new, specialty applications, for conformal power storage that would be much more easily and cheaply made than with other technologies; particularly those subject to sudden uncontrolled ignition when the casing is breached. A high value specialty application, requiring smaller packages, might be a good intermediate to get to vehicle scale production of a standard 12v battery competitive with Lead Acid or Lithium cells in mainstream transportation.

Have you considered a crowdfunding campaign like Kickstarter?
What I think you need is a social media savvy young person to fluff your marketing a bit (I absolutely Love your channel, but I have more than a 30 second attention span and find your work and your brilliance fascinating and I mostly can follow what you're talking about having grown up on rather dry and in depth public educational television so...)

If I thought I could manage it well, I would offer to do it myself, but I'm not the right kind of savvy.  I could do the pitch, but I don't have the editing or social media chops to get it to go viral-  and I DO believe a green, rivals lithium ion, brilliant battery solution could absolutely catch the public imagination, thus rendering skeezy deals with profiteers irrelevant. 

But trust me, you need somebody young and social media savvy that believes in what you're doing, and understands how crowdfunding works (and watch out for the site cost, and funding rewards cost in time and money.  that catches people unawares), and you can absolutely make this happen.

author: @johnallenrichter
text: Robert, love your videos. But your battery is already outdated.  Dr. Goodenough - creator of the original lithium ion battery - is promising a new safe solid state battery with a glass electrolyte that will handle 150,000 charge cycles and no need for battery management.  Ford is dedicating 20 billion for in-house R&D and production of solid state batteries, and a couple of other companies are following suit.  There is a Japanese company also promising similar results.  There is a company in the US currently producing graphene foam batteries which looks promising to replace the current lead acid markets.  Perhaps you could reach out to them for possible partnership.  I think it is SSD Inc but not sure about that.  Otherwise I'm certain most investors are waiting to invest in the new solid state arena.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
MY interest is building the MACHINES which would streamline and make cost effective the mass production process!

Also, if at all possible, make it possible to do those at a small, portable scale.

The problem is, typically, to make something in a really cost effective manner often requires large, bulky, expensive machinery, so the challenge is to make it with something small and yet at a rate which makes logical sense based on the potential user need.

In other words, its a lot like comparing a giant offset newspaper press to a small home printer.

One is very portable, relatively inexpensive to obtain, is easily replaced if it stops working, and pretty much anybody can get one, but on the other hand, it is slow, effectively very pricey, and very labor intensive.

One produces large volumes rapidly with very little human interaction, but is huge, costs a whole lot up front, and most people can not afford to even maintain if the parts go bad, and on top of that, they are very complicated to learn how to operate or maintain.

So then: how to make something small, durable, portable [if not 'light weight'], simple, reliable [not jamming, getting too dirty, out of alignment or clogging up], low cost, fast, and low labor intensity [mostly automated].

The gov comes in, finds out what your doing, how to do it then supports the university's so they can claim its their product for years of their own research.
What Rob is doing is the best approach. Build up an online presents and be known for creating the product.

When enough people know of you your become like the tamigotchi product in that many will be aware of you and your product.

Keep going Rob, there are many who want you to succeed as well as fail.

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text: Very bad idea. Keep everything in house as much as possible, even the development  of the manufacturing  side which itself could have patentable value. If you have to farm out, split the development across trusted individuals who work for YOU and even then dont offer full disclosure of the product. That way you can retain manufacturing innovation rights. Couple that with a shed load of NDA's and you might stand a chance of not being ripped off but that's no guarantee. I used to work for the company that produced the well known self setting silicone rubber product. They had worldwide patents but in the end they offer little protection. In situations like this, it pays to be a little paranoid.... 😋
